title: Sunday repairs
summary: Every Sunday, Mr Bao fixes small broken things from his neighbours' houses. He does not need any of them.
body:
Every Sunday morning, Mr Bao sits on his balcony with a box of tools. The box is old. The tools are older. By eight, the neighbours start to bring him things.

Mrs Ha brings a kettle that does not boil. The Linh family brings a child's bicycle with a loose chain. Old Mr Cao brings a clock that runs five minutes fast every hour. Sometimes children bring broken toys. Mr Bao takes everything and says, 'I will look at it.'

He is seventy-two. He worked as an electrician for forty years. He retired ten years ago. His wife died last spring. His children live in another city.

He does not need a kettle, a child's bicycle, or a clock. But every Sunday, he repairs them. He does not charge any money. He just hands the thing back on Monday morning.

Last month, a young woman moved into the building. She did not know about Mr Bao. She saw the queue of neighbours and asked her friend, 'What is happening?'

'Oh, that is Mr Bao,' her friend said. 'He fixes everything.'

'And what does he get?' the young woman asked.

'A reason to be on the balcony every Sunday,' her friend said. 'A reason to make tea. A reason for somebody to knock on his door.'

The young woman went home and looked at her kitchen. Her toaster was broken. She was about to buy a new one. Instead, she put the toaster in a bag. Next Sunday, she walked downstairs.

annotations.meaningVi: không thu tiền, không lấy phí
sắp sửa (làm gì đó), chuẩn bị làm
chạy nhanh hơn năm phút (đồng hồ chạy sai giờ)
mất vào mùa xuân năm ngoái (quá khứ đơn: 'died' + thời điểm cụ thể)

grammarNotes.title: Past simple vs. simple present for routines
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hiện tại đơn mô tả việc lặp lại hàng tuần: 'Every Sunday morning, Mr Bao sits on his small balcony.' Quá khứ đơn mô tả việc đã xảy ra trong quá khứ: 'His wife died last spring.' / 'A young woman moved into the building.' Bài tận dụng cả hai để xây dựng nhân vật.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The simple present describes Mr Bao's weekly routine: 'Every Sunday morning, Mr Bao sits on his small balcony.' Past simple describes finished events: 'His wife died last spring.' / 'A young woman moved into the building.' The story uses both to build the character.

translationVi:
Sáng Chủ nhật nào ông Bảo cũng ngồi ngoài ban công với một hộp dụng cụ. Hộp cũ rồi. Dụng cụ còn cũ hơn nữa. Đến tám giờ, hàng xóm bắt đầu mang đồ đến.

Cô Hà mang đến cái ấm không sôi. Gia đình nhà Linh mang xe đạp trẻ con bị lỏng xích. Bác Cao mang cái đồng hồ cứ mỗi giờ lại chạy nhanh năm phút. Đôi khi trẻ con mang đồ chơi hỏng đến. Ông Bảo nhận hết và nói: 'Để tôi xem.'

Ông bảy mươi hai. Ông từng làm thợ điện bốn mươi năm. Ông nghỉ hưu mười năm trước. Vợ ông mất mùa xuân năm ngoái. Các con ông sống ở thành phố khác.

Ông không cần cái ấm, cái xe đạp trẻ con, hay cái đồng hồ. Nhưng Chủ nhật nào ông cũng sửa. Ông không lấy tiền. Sáng thứ Hai, ông chỉ đưa đồ trả lại.

Tháng trước, một cô gái trẻ chuyển vào tòa nhà. Cô không biết về ông Bảo. Cô thấy hàng người chờ và hỏi bạn: 'Có chuyện gì vậy?'

'À, ông Bảo đấy,' bạn cô nói. 'Ông sửa tất cả mọi thứ.'

'Vậy ông nhận được gì?' cô gái hỏi.

'Một lý do để ngồi ngoài ban công Chủ nhật,' bạn cô nói. 'Một lý do để pha trà. Một lý do để có người gõ cửa nhà mình.'

Cô gái về nhà và nhìn cái bếp của mình. Máy nướng bánh của cô đã hỏng. Cô đang định mua cái mới. Thay vì vậy, cô bỏ máy nướng vào túi. Chủ nhật sau, cô đi xuống tầng.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Bao do every Sunday morning?
options: He sits on his balcony with a box of tools and fixes things for neighbours.
questions.explanation: 'Every Sunday morning, Mr Bao sits on his small balcony with a box of tools.'

questions.prompt: What does Mrs Ha bring to Mr Bao?
options: A kettle that does not boil
questions.explanation: 'Mrs Ha brings a kettle that does not boil.'

questions.prompt: What is wrong with Old Mr Cao's clock?
options: It does not work at all.
It runs five minutes fast every hour.
questions.explanation: 'Old Mr Cao brings a clock that runs five minutes fast every hour.'
'He worked as an electrician for forty years.'

questions.prompt: How much money does Mr Bao charge for the repairs?
Who is the young woman in the story?
options: A new neighbour who just moved into the building
questions.explanation: 'A young woman moved into the building.'

questions.prompt: What does the friend say Mr Bao gets in return for fixing things?
options: A reason to be on the balcony, to make tea, and for somebody to knock on his door
questions.explanation: 'A reason to be on the balcony every Sunday. A reason to make tea. A reason for somebody to knock on his door.'

questions.prompt: What was the young woman planning to do with her broken toaster before this conversation?
options: Throw it away and buy a new one
Bring it to Mr Bao immediately
questions.explanation: 'Her toaster was broken. She was about to buy a new one.'

questions.prompt: What does the young woman do at the end?
options: She puts the toaster in a bag and goes downstairs to Mr Bao next Sunday.
She gives the toaster to her friend.
She moves out of the building.
questions.explanation: 'Instead, she put the toaster in a bag. Next Sunday, she walked downstairs.'
questions.hint: The last sentences of the story.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the story?
options: Doing something for others can also be doing something for yourself.
Tools last longer if you keep them in a box.
questions.explanation: The friend explains that the repairs give Mr Bao reason for company and routine — the help works both ways.
questions.hint: Look at the friend's answer about what Mr Bao 'gets.'

blanks.explanation: Mr Bao sits with a box of tools.
The Linh family brings a child's bicycle.
He worked as an electrician for forty years.
She saw the queue of neighbours.
'What does he get?' — what does he receive?
A reason to make tea for someone who comes.
She was about to buy a new toaster.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Bao's repairs are partly for the neighbours and partly for himself — they give him company and routine. Is there a small task you do, supposedly for others, that quietly does as much for you? What would change in your week if you stopped?
